coredata.dc:title: A convenient green method to synthesize luminescent carbon dots from edible carrot and its application in bioimaging and preparation of nanocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Jayanthi, Muruganandam
Megarajan, Sengan
Subramaniyan, Siva Bala
Kamlekar, Ravi Kanth
Veerappan, Anbazhagan
coredata.dc:description: A simple single-stage method, based on refluxing edible carrot with aqueous trisodium phosphate (TSP) has been reported to synthesize blue color luminescent carbon dots (CDs). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showed that CDs are almost spherical in shape and the size is around 3–8 nm. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and 1H NMR spectroscopy study revealed that the carbohydrates present in the carrot undergone carbonization to produce luminescent CDs. Optimization study indicate that refluxing 5 g of carrot in 80 mM TSP aqueous solution for 120 min is adequate to produce highly luminescent blue CDs. The CDs produced are used as luminophore to image bacteria through fluorescence microscopy. CDs have membrane good permeability and minimum toxicity against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. The CDs capability for direct reduction of silver ions to elemental silver (Ag0) and gold ions to elemental gold (Au0) without additional reducing and stabilizing agent was demonstrated. The resulting Ag and Au nanoparticles have a size of 8–22 nm and 5–15 nm, respectively. The catalytic activity of nanoparticles in the hydrogenation reaction was investigated. The results suggest that the nanoparticles had high catalytic activity in the sodium borohydride mediated hydrogenation of nitroaromatic compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carrot
Trisodium phosphate
Bioimaging
Nanoparticles
Nanocatalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Representative procedure for hydrogenation of nitroaromatics

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology, Science and Engineering Research Board

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: SM and SBS gratefully acknowledge Teaching Assistantship from SASTRA Deemed University. We thank DST FIST (SB/FST/ETI-331/2013) and the central research facility (R&M/0021/SCBT-007/2012-13), SASTRA University for the infrastructure. RKK ( YSS/2014/000021 ) acknowledges Department of Science and Technology, Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Government of India for the financial support. We thank VIT for HRTEM facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) 3-D fluorescence spectra, (B) Excitation and emission spectra of C-dots. Inset corresponds to photograph of C-dots without (i) and with UV-light irradiation (ii). (C) TEM images of C-dots and (D) High resolution TEM image show the lattice pattern.

(A) FTIR spectra of carrot extract (black line) and carbon dots (blue line). Arrows indicate the peaks correspond to the functional groups present in CE and CDs. (B) Whole survey XPS spectrum of carrot CDs, (C) C1s XPS spectrum of carrot CDs.
1H NMR spectra of (A) carrot extract and (B) carbon dots.
Optimization of CDs preparation. (A) Fluorescence of CDs collected at various time points, and (B) Fluorescence of CDs formed at various concentrations of TSP.
Bright field transmission and fluorescence image of E. coli and S. aureus cells labeled with CDs.

(A) UV–visible spectra of (a) CDs, (b) AgNPs, and (c) AuNPs. Inset corresponds to the photographs of 4× diluted AgNPs (b) and AuNPs (c). (B) EDX spectrum of AgNPs. (C) EDX spectrum of AuNPs. The peak corresponds to Cu is attributed to the copper grid used for measurement.
TEM images of (A) AgNPs and (B) AuNPs. High resolution TEM of (C) AgNPs and (D) AuNPs shows the fringe pattern. Inset corresponds to the SAED pattern.
(A) UV–visible spectra of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 4-nitrophenol + NaBH4, and the reduction product 4-aminophenol (4-AP) (B) Kinetic trace. The k was determined by linear fit.
Induction time (t0) and rate constant (k) for the reduction of nitroaromatics.

head.ce:title: A convenient green method to synthesize luminescent carbon dots from edible carrot and its application in bioimaging and preparation of nanocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemical and Biotechnology, SASTRA Deemed University, Thanjavur 613401, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advance Sciences, VIT, Vellore 632014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple single-stage method, based on refluxing edible carrot with aqueous trisodium phosphate (TSP) has been reported to synthesize blue color luminescent carbon dots (CDs). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showed that CDs are almost spherical in shape and the size is around 3–8 nm. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and 1H NMR spectroscopy study revealed that the carbohydrates present in the carrot undergone carbonization to produce luminescent CDs. Optimization study indicate that refluxing 5 g of carrot in 80 mM TSP aqueous solution for 120 min is adequate to produce highly luminescent blue CDs. The CDs produced are used as luminophore to image bacteria through fluorescence microscopy. CDs have membrane good permeability and minimum toxicity against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. The CDs capability for direct reduction of silver ions to elemental silver (Ag0) and gold ions to elemental gold (Au0) without additional reducing and stabilizing agent was demonstrated. The resulting Ag and Au nanoparticles have a size of 8–22 nm and 5–15 nm, respectively. The catalytic activity of nanoparticles in the hydrogenation reaction was investigated. The results suggest that the nanoparticles had high catalytic activity in the sodium borohydride mediated hydrogenation of nitroaromatic compounds.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon nanomaterials with size below 10 nm are called as carbon dots (CDs), which have excellent optical and chemical properties [1,2]. Unlike traditional organic fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots, fluorescent CDs have the advantage of excellent photoluminescence, greater photostability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [3]. The unique features of CDs have been explored in diverse field including bioimaging, sensing, catalysis, and biomedicine [4–6]. CDs have been used to develop fluorometric assays for enzymes including β-galactosidase, butyrylcholinesterase, α-glucosidase and so forth [7–10]. Hence, varieties of methods are developed to synthesis CDs which includes laser ablation, hydrothermal treatments, pyrolysis, acidic treatment, alkaline oxidation, electrochemical exfoliation, arc discharge and microwave heating [11–15]. These methods are advantageous in terms of CDs preparation, but the sophisticated instrument requirement and complex post-treatment demands for an easy, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly method. Hydrothermal route was the most popular in the green synthesis of CDs, which uses natural bio-resources, such as banana, orange peel, papaya, apple juice, winter melon, cow milk, Soy milk for the preparation of CDs [16–22]. The basic requirement for the hydrothermal technique is hydrothermal apparatus and high temperature furnace, which is not commonly available in all laboratories.

In this paper, we reported a facile green method to synthesis CDs by exploiting commonly available laboratory equipment and chemicals. The CDs were prepared through one pot reflux method by using edible carrot as a carbon source and aqueous solution of trisodium phosphate as a catalyst. As-prepared CDs have excellent blue emission under UV light irradiation and showed fluorescence emission at 450 nm. The suitability of CDs for bioimaging was demonstrated in bacteria model, Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. Recent developments showed that CDs derived from different source are useful in the preparation of metal nanoparticles [23–25]. The exponential increases in the utilization of metal nanoparticles in diverse filed encourages researcher to develop facile, cost-effective and reliable green method for the production of nanomaterials [26–29]. So far, there is no report about the synthesis of silver or gold nanoparticles using CDs derived from edible carrot. Herein, we demonstrated that our CDs have the ability to reduce Ag+ to Ag0 and Au3+ to Au0, and also stabilize them from agglomeration. The formation of silver (AgNPs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) was confirmed by energy dispersive X-ray diffraction (EDAX) and also by characteristic surface plasmon resonance of the nanoparticle. The morphology and size was judged by transmission electron microscopy. The catalytic application of CDs stabilized AgNPs and AuNPs were demonstrated in sodium borohydride mediated hydrogenation reaction of nitroaromatics compounds. In summary, CDs synthesized in this study have important application values in the field of bioimaging and nanocatalyst.

Edible carrot was procured from the local supermarket. Trisodium phosphate, and sodium borohydride were purchased from Merck, India. Silver nitrate was purchased from Fisher Scientific, India. Gold chloride was obtained from Alfa Aesar. All other reagents were of analytical grade obtained from the local supplier. Double distilled water was used throughout the work unless otherwise specified.

About 5 g of carrot was choked into very fine pieces and added into round bottom flask containing 25 mL of 100 mM trisodium phosphate aqueous solution (TSP). The round bottom flask fitted with a condenser was placed on a laboratory heating mantel and refluxed for 3 h. The color of the solution turns from colorless to pale yellow and then finally to brown, indicating the process of carbonization to yield carbon dots. Irradiating the obtained solution with UV lamp at 360 nm confirms the formation of blue fluorescence CDs. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured in Jasco-FP8200 spectrofluorimeter with slit width set at 5 nm for both excitation and emission monochromators. UV–Visible absorption spectrum was monitored in Thermo Scientific Evolution 201 spectrophotometer operated at 1 nm resolution. The size and morphology were evaluated in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-JEM 1011, Japan) operating at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. The dried powder of CDs obtained through lyophilization was subjected to FTIR spectroscopy measurements. FTIR was recorded on a Perkin Elmer spectrum-one instrument in the diffuse reflectance mode at a resolution of 1 cm−1 in KBr pellet. 1H NMR was measured in a Bruker 300 MHz instrument. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on Thermofisher scientific, UK, Kα surface analysis model using Al-X-ray in a wide range of 0–1350 eV.

The possibility of using CDs to live cell imagining was investigated using Gram negative bacteria, E. coli and Gram positive bacteria, S. aureus. For imaging studies, overnight cultured bacterial cells with optical density of 0.5–0.6 at 660 nm were incubated with 1 mL of freshly prepared CDs for 2 h in dark at 37 °C. After incubation, the cell suspension was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 10 min and washed three times with PBS buffer. After washing and centrifugation, the pellet was collected and suspended in PBS buffer. A drop of bacterial cell suspension was placed on a cover slip and visualized through a fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse) using blue filter.

Typically, 200 μL of C-dots was added to 9.8 mL of water containing either 1 mM silver nitrate or gold chloride. The resultant solution was left undisturbed for 12 h. The change in the solution color indicates the formation of NPs. UV–Visible spectra of the NPs were measured in Thermo Scientific Evolution 201 spectrophotometer. The size and morphology of the NPs was determined using FEI-Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN High resolution TEM (HRTEM). Elemental composition of the NPs was analyzed by Bruker XFlash 6 T130 EDX Detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Representative procedure for hydrogenation of nitroaromatics
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrocompounds (4-nitrophenol, 4-nitroaniline, and 4-methyl-2-nitroaniline) showing an absorbance in the visible region was selected to follow the hydrogenation reaction. The absorbance of 4-nitrophenol, 4-nitroaniline, and 4-methyl-2-nitroaniline were observed at 400 nm, 408 nm, and 427 nm, respectively. The hydrogenation reaction was performed by adding 10 mg of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and 100 μL of NPs (1 mM) to 100 μM of nitroaromatic compounds dissolved in 3 mL of water. The change in absorbance with respect to time was recorded to determine the kinetics of the reaction.

Heating carbohydrates with strong alkali like sodium hydroxide (NaOH) is known for producing carbon dots (CDs) [4]. However, NaOH is highly corrosive and causes serious damage when not handled safely. A better replacement for NaOH may be trisodium phosphate (TSP), because even 1% solution is sufficiently alkaline with pH 12. TSP is not toxic and approved as safer food additives in European Union. Generally, sodium phosphates are used as acidity regulators and have the collective E number, E339 [30]. Having known that edible carrot has a rich source of phytonutrients including free sugars [31], we attempted to synthesize CDs using carrot and TSP. Boiling carrot pieces in water for 3 to 5 h produce a pale yellow carrot extract (CE) solution. Irradiating CE solution with UV-lamp showed no fluorescence. Phytochemical screening of the aqueous CE revealed the presence of carbohydrates, and amino acids. At the same time, refluxing carrot pieces in 100 mM TSP aqueous solution produced a brown color solution which showed strong blue fluorescence, indicating the formation of CDs through the process of carbonization. The optimum peak excitation and emission wavelength for CDs was determined by 3-D fluorescence method (Fig. 1A). The emission spectrum measured for CDs while varying the excitation wavelength show emission maximum at 450 nm, which is the characteristic emission of blue luminescent fluorophore [5]. The 2-D excitation spectra measured by setting emission maximum at 450 nm showed an excitation maximum at 365 nm (Fig. 1B). Transmission electron microscopy image revealed that the size distribution of CDs derived from carrot was narrow in the range of 3–8 nm (Fig. 1C). The high resolution TEM image reveals the lattice structure of synthesized CDs (Fig. 1D).

The water dispersibility of the CDs obtained through the carbonization of carrot phytonutrients was good, suggesting that CDs were protected by some functional groups. The presence of functional group was investigated by FTIR, the FTIR spectrum of CE and CDs are shown in Fig. 2A. A broad peak associated with OH and NH stretching was observed at 3250 cm−1. The peak corresponds to CH stretching was observed at 2926 and 2850 cm−1 and the corresponding bending vibrations of CH were observed at 1405 cm−1. The absorption peak associated with pyranose form of sugar was observed at 1043 cm−1. The peak at 1575 cm−1 is attributed to CC vibrations. Interestingly, CDs showed a sharp peak at 1575 cm−1 as compared to the carrot extract. At the same time, the CO peak observed at 1740 and 1650 cm−1 in the carrot extract was completely lost in CDs, indicate that the carbonyl group containing phytonutrients undergo carbonization and form more sp2 hybridized carbon. The functional group present on the surface of CDs was further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The whole XPS survey spectrum of the CDs in Fig. 2B clearly showed peaks with binding energy at 285.08, 400.08, and 532.08 eV, which are originated from C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The exclusive C1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 2C clearly demonstrated three carbon states at 284.78, 286.18, and 287.98 eV, which can be attributed to CC/CC, CO and CN, respectively. These results are consistent with FTIR study and suggest that CC might result in a series of emissive traps between π and π* to produce blue emitting CDs.

Besides FTIR and XPS, we also performed 1H NMR with CE and CDs (Fig. 3 ). The 1H NMR spectra of CE and CDs showed a broad multiplet between 3 and 4 ppm, indicating the existence of mixture of saccharides. The resonance corresponds to the protons attached to methyl and methylene moieties were observed between 1 and 2 ppm. The resonance at 5.2 and 8.2 ppm is attributed to the vinylic and aromatic protons. The chemical shift at 2.2 ppm is attributed to the protons attached to carbonyl residues. On comparing the 1H NMR spectra of CE and CDs, we observed that the peak at 2.2 ppm decreases considerably with concomitant increases in the peak at 8.3 ppm (Fig. 3). This can be explained by considering the loss of carbonyl residues and formation of aromatic clusters. Noteworthy that the number of signals observed between 3 and 4 ppm in the CE was reduced considerably in CDs, indicating that some sugar residues undergone carbonization. Based on the above evidence and also reports from others [32,33], we propose that phytonutrients undergo dehydration, decomposition in the presence of TSP and the products formed may undergo polymerization to form clusters. Once these clusters reach the critical supersaturating concentration, they will undergo carbonization and aromatization, resulting in nuclear burst of aromatic clusters to produce blue color emitting CDs. The quantum yield of carrot CDs was determined as 4.56% using quinine sulfate as the standard reference [8]. The reported quantum yield is comparable to CDs obtained from natural resources, such as starch (2.46%) [34], citrus pectin (1.1%) [35], natural gas soot (0.43%) [36], green tea (6.8%) [37], banana juice (8.95%) [16].

Having observed that carbon dots are formed in our reaction, we optimized the reaction time and concentration of TSP to acquire highly luminescent CDs. The impact of reaction time on the formation of CDs was investigated at fixed weight of carrot pieces (5 g) and fixed concentration of TSP (100 mM). As shown in Fig. 4A, carbonization was commence at 15 min and reaches maximum at 120 min. Prolonging the reaction time for 300 min does not improvise the fluorescence emission intensity, indicating that the carbonizable phytonutrients responsible for the formation of CDs was all consumed in less than 120 min. Next, the effect of TSP on the carbonization of CE was studied through fixing the time (120 min) and weight of carrot (5 g). As shown in Fig. 4B, the concentrations of TSP have been instrumental in the formation of CDs, which was estimated in the form of fluorescence emission intensity. However, the fluorescence intensity drops down significantly above 100 mM TSP. This can be explained by considering the decomposition of those phytonutrients stabilizing the CDs. Thus, we suggest that 80–100 mM TSP is an ideal concentrations and refluxing for 120 min is sufficient to produce highly luminescent blue CDs from the edible carrot.

We explored the possibility of using CDs for imaging live cells by fluorescence microscopy. Gram negative bacteria, E. coli and Gram positive bacteria, S. aureus was chosen as a model organism for bioimaging. For fluorescence imaging experiments, bacteria cells pre-exposed to CDs were washed with PBS buffer and drop-cast onto a cover slip. On viewing through a fluorescence microscope using blue filter, luminescent blue cells were observed. As inferred from Fig. 5 , CDs basically label the cell membrane and cytoplasm of both E. coli and S. aureus presumably through passive endocytosis. The rod-shaped morphology of E. coli and cocci-shaped morphology of S. aureus was clearly visible through labeling with blue fluorescence CDs (Fig. 5). Noteworthy that, CDs labels both Gram negative and Gram positive bacteria irrespective of their difference in the outer membrane composition. However, further studies are required to define the mechanism of labeling.

A convenient method to synthesis carbon dots from edible carrot was reported using commonly available chemical and labwares. Optimum synthetic parameters have been found, and the prepared CDs have been characterized by TEM, FTIR, 1H NMR and fluorescence spectroscopy. It is found that 80 mM TSP is sufficient to produce CDs from 5 g of carrot. FTIR and 1H NMR analysis revealed that the carbonyl containing phytonutrients undergone carbonization to yield blue luminescent CDs. The application of CDs in optical imaging was demonstrated in Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial models. The prepared CDs have an ability to reduce metal ions like silver and gold to nanoparticles and stabilize them against aggregation. As prepared AgNPs and AuNPs display excellent catalytic activity as evidence from the kinetics of hydrogenation of nitroaromatics. The simplicity of the CDs synthesis ensures their promising applications in the preparation of efficient nanocatalyst and in the development of cost-effective imaging agents.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SM and SBS gratefully acknowledge Teaching Assistantship from SASTRA Deemed University. We thank DST FIST (SB/FST/ETI-331/2013) and the central research facility (R&M/0021/SCBT-007/2012-13), SASTRA University for the infrastructure. RKK (YSS/2014/000021) acknowledges Department of Science and Technology, Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Government of India for the financial support. We thank VIT for HRTEM facility.

Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups

Electrochemical synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from glycine for highly sensitive detection of hemoglobin
One-step synthesis of surface passivated carbon nanodots by microwave assisted pyrolysis for enhanced multicolor photoluminescence and bioimaging
A new hydrothermal refluxing route to strong fluorescent carbon dots and its application as fluorescent imaging agent
A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents

Application of cow milk-derived carbon dots/Ag NPs composite as the antibacterial agent
One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells

Easy synthesis of photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots from winter melon forbio-imaging
Growth and stabilization of silver nanoparticles on carbon dots and sensing application
